does: This paper identifies and demonstrates CodeSpear, a novel jailbreak attack that exploits Grammar-Constrained Decoding (GCD)—a safety-oriented technique widely used to enforce syntactic validity in LLM-generated code—to manipulate models into producing malicious code. The authors conduct experiments showing that applying seemingly benign code grammar constraints can effectively bypass existing safety mechanisms. To mitigate this vulnerability, they propose CodeShield, a safety alignment approach that teaches LLMs to generate structurally valid but semantically harmless honeypot code when subjected to attacker-controlled grammar constraints, ensuring models maintain safe behavior regardless of the grammar imposed during decoding.
connects: This work builds on longstanding research into both LLM safety alignment and code generation reliability. It extends prior work on adversarial attacks and jailbreaking techniques against LLMs, which have explored prompt injection, context manipulation, and other input-based attacks. Concurrently, it responds to the growing literature on Grammar-Constrained Decoding as a reliability mechanism for code generation, which itself emerged from earlier work on controlled generation and structured prediction. The paper connects to safety alignment research that addresses training-time defenses against adversarial inputs, particularly methods like Constitutional AI and instruction-following alignment. It also relates to studies examining how safety mechanisms can introduce unintended vulnerabilities or create new attack surfaces.
enables: This research opens multiple future directions in securing code-generation systems. It motivates deeper investigation into whether other reliability-oriented decoding techniques harbor similar jailbreak vulnerabilities, potentially requiring systematic auditing of constraint-based generation methods. The work enables development of more robust safety training approaches specifically designed for constrained decoding scenarios, where defenders must account for attacker-controlled structural constraints. It points toward new evaluation frameworks for assessing LLM safety under diverse deployment conditions and technical constraints. The honeypot code generation strategy also suggests broader defenses where models learn to satisfy formal constraints while preserving safety semantics, applicable beyond code to other high-stakes domains requiring both structural validity and adversarial robustness.
